elements.sent-2411-001: In this paper, we propose a cohomology-based Gromov-Hausdorff ultrametric to quantify structural similarity between two molecular structures.
elements.sent-2411-002: We represent molecules as simplicial complexes and compute their first-order cohomology vector spaces to capture intrinsic topological invariants that encode loop structures.
elements.sent-2411-003: These vector spaces are then equipped with a chosen distance measure.
elements.sent-2411-004: This metrization step allows us to compute the Gromov-Hausdorff ultrametrics between two cohomology vector spaces and thus quantify their structural dissimilarity.
elements.sent-2411-005: We showcase the application of the proposed methodology using catalyst structures and organic-inorganic halide perovskite (OIHP) structures.
elements.sent-2411-006: The results demonstrate the effectiveness of this approach in clustering various molecular structures.. }\DIFaddend \DIFaddbegin \end{abstract.
elements.sent-2411-007: Geometric theories and tools used here include, e.g., (discrete) Exterior Calculus, Laplace Operators, Optimal Transport, Parametric Surfaces, and Geometric Flows, discrete Ricci curvatures like Ollivier and Forman Ricci curvature, Geometric Modelling and Signal Processing, Graph and Simplex Neural Networks, Conformal and Combinatorial Geometry, Dimension Reduction Techniques, Geometric Deep Learning, etc., and have proven to often yield stronger results than mere topological ones.
elements.sent-2411-008: However, though already quite powerful, usually one can only extract limited information about the homology groups of the objects in question, e.g., often indeed not much more than their Betti numbers.
elements.sent-2411-009: It is based on the fact that in the case where the coefficients take values in the real numbers, via the combinatorial Hodge Laplacian there are, described in detail below, several natural norms and distances available which, in turn, then do yield stronger information than methods from topological data analysis can provide (but the basic idea indeed pertains to any field of coefficients or situations where distances can be introduced).
elements.sent-2411-010: However, the latter are in general very hard to compute, and to show that this approach is indeed amenable and applicable to typical 'real-life' questions of molecular data analysis, we therefore perform an important second step: we turn $H^p(K_A)$ and $H^p(K_B)$ into ultrametric ones, thus obtain respective dendogram representatives of them, and then simply have to compute the ultrametric Gromov-Hausdorff distance of the dendograms.
elements.sent-2411-011: Researchers leverage this ability to identify small molecules possessing desirable drug-like properties in drug discovery, where interactions among these molecules are governed by intricate chemical and biological processes \cite{maggiora2014molecular .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Such tasks pose a tremendous challenge to human capability in pattern recognition, especially when attempting to relate molecular structures to their functions.
elements.sent-2411-013: A crucial aspect of finding patterns in molecular data is how molecules are represented, often referred to as molecular representation or featurization.
elements.sent-2411-014: Currently, there are thousands of descriptors developed that describe molecular properties and encode structural features, most of which can be computed using commercial packages \cite{gallegos2006mini .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Each of the existing descriptors encodes different information and is optimized for specific applications.
elements.sent-2411-016: A set of descriptors can be combined into a molecular feature vector, which can be used as an input for statistical modeling.
elements.sent-2411-017: For example, real-valued descriptors, such as molecular weight or polarity, assign a single value to a molecule, thereby providing a global summary statistic.
elements.sent-2411-018: Driven by the need for rapid retrieval of chemical database information, key-based bitstrings descriptors were developed, where each bit represents the presence or absence of a molecular feature or substructure \cite{willett2005searching, ahrens1988customisation .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Growing interest in structure-activity studies and molecular similarity analysis in the 80s and 90s led to the development of topological fingerprints based on the adjacency of atom pairs \cite{carhart1985atom or local neighborhood \cite{rogers2010extended .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Advances in QSAR and X-ray crystallographic structures combined with enhanced computation power propelled the design of fingerprints based on three-dimensional geometric distances and structures such as rapid overlay of chemical structures (ROCS) \cite{rush2005shape .}
elements.sent-2411-021: These three-dimensional descriptors offers new opportunities for 3D-QSAR and similarity analysis.
elements.sent-2411-022: Expert chemists often select molecular descriptors based on their domain knowledge of the causal relationships and mechanics underlying the observation.
elements.sent-2411-023: However, when there is a lack of fundamental understanding of the mechanics or when the space of descriptors is too vast, researchers are increasingly turning to machine learning pipelines to select features and design molecular representations.
elements.sent-2411-024: It should be expressive enough to capture the rich chemical space and distinguish subtle differences.
elements.sent-2411-025: Additionally, it should consistently generate the same input regardless of the labeling of atoms or the rotation or translation of the molecules \cite{todeschini2009molecular, chuang2020learning .}
elements.sent-2411-026: In the last decade, mathematical approaches including algebraic topology and graph theory have found manifold applications in molecular representation \cite{wasserman2018topological, bubenik:2015, dey2018protein, wei2022hodge, nguyen2020review .}
elements.sent-2411-027: These mathematical tools allow expressive representation of molecules based on their three-dimensional connectivity and structures.
elements.sent-2411-028: Such representation enables computation of the topological and geometric invariants.
elements.sent-2411-029: These invariants are highly abstract and robust to noise and rototranslations, making them suitable inputs for machine learning models \cite{wasserman2018topological, chazal2021introduction .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Machine learning models employing the aforementioned mathematical approaches have demonstrated outstanding performance in D3R Grand Challenges, a global competition on computer-aided drug design \cite{nguyen2019mathematical .}
elements.sent-2411-031: After selecting a molecular representation tailored for a specific application, similarity between molecules can be measured based on their features.
elements.sent-2411-032: Similarity studies have found widespread application in toxicology , drug design, and database retrieval to assess whether chemicals possess similar biochemical activity or physicochemical properties \cite{gallegos2006mini, willett2009similarity, bender2004molecular, dean2012molecular .}
elements.sent-2411-033: This practice is underpinned by the Similar Property Principle, which states that molecules sharing structural similarities are likely to possess similar properties \cite{johnson1990concepts .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Therefore, a molecular representation capable of capturing rich structural information is of key importance for similarity analysis.
elements.sent-2411-035: Since similarity is a relative term with respect to a notion of distance, another critical aspect in similarity analysis is the choice of similarity or distance measure for a chosen representation.
elements.sent-2411-036: Numerous quantitative methods for measuring structural similarity based on molecular constitution, such as presence and absence of fragments, and 3D atomic arrangements have been proposed to date \cite{nikolova2003approaches, gallegos2006mini .}
elements.sent-2411-037: For example, bit-string features representing the presence of substructures are usually compared with Tanimoto coefficient, $\tau = N_{A\and B /(N_A + N_B - N_{A\and B )$, where $N_A$ (or $N_A$) is the number of substructures present in A (or B), and $N_{A\and B}$ is the number of substructures present in both A and B \cite{bajusz2015tanimoto .}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Some popular methods for analyzing 3D molecular similarity include Comparative Molecular Field Analysis (CoMFA), which samples the 3D molecular field on a grid of points, and Quantum Similarity Indices (QSI), which compare electron density distributions using quantum mechanics computations.
elements.sent-2411-039: The issue with these methods is that comparing three-dimensional atomic arrangements typically requires aligning molecules or selecting comparable sampling points which can be computationally intensive.
elements.sent-2411-040: Some alignment-free methods have been proposed to capture 3D configurations.
elements.sent-2411-041: These methods encompass Molecular Moment Analysis (CoMMA), which utilize lower-order moments of molecular distribution \cite{silverman1996comparative , as well as more recent similarity measures based on persistent homology, which leverage topological characterization \cite{xia2018persistent .}}
elements.sent-2411-042: The Gromov-Hausdorff distance ($d_{GH}$) \cite{edwards1975structure, gromov1981groups quantifies dissimilarity between two metric spaces, each consisting of a set of elements together with a distance measure.}
elements.sent-2411-043: The Gromov-Hausdorff distance possess many desirable mathematical properties and has been widely used in differential geometry and applied algebraic topology for shape matching, comparison, and characterizations \cite{memoli2007use, bronstein2010gromov, chazal2009gromov .}
elements.sent-2411-044: In many of the previous research utilizing the Gromov-Hausdorff distance, it generally serves as a global measure of structural similarity.
elements.sent-2411-045: Our aim is to capture localized characteristics, such as the positioning of loops, which are strongly correlated with chemical and physical properties.
elements.sent-2411-046: To achieve this, we introduce a framework that represents molecular structures based on their first-order cohomology vector spaces, which reveal loop patterns within the structures.
elements.sent-2411-047: When computing $d_{GH}$, although many lower bounds can be achieved in polynomial time \cite{chazal2009gromov, memoli2012some , computing $d_{GH}$ between arbitrary finite metric spaces has been proven to be NP-hard, as it leads to quadratic assignment problems \cite{memoli2012some .}}
elements.sent-2411-048: For computational tractability, we adopt an ultrametric variant of the Gromov-Hausdorff distance , called the Gromov-Hausdorff ultrametric ($u_{GH}$)\cite{zarichnyi2005gromov, memoli2021gromov by converting the cohomology generator spaces into ultrametric spaces.}
elements.sent-2411-049: The main contributions of this work are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-050: We introduce the first workflow employing cohomology-based Gromov-Hausdorff ultrametric between to assess structural similarity between molecular structures.
elements.sent-2411-051: This approach enables us to effectively capture local topological structures, such as loop arrangement, which significantly influences molecular properties.
elements.sent-2411-052: We demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed workflow through clustering tasks, specifically focusing on catalyst data and organic-inorganic halide perovskites (OIHP) data.
elements.sent-2411-053: The rest of this paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-054: In the next section, we will provide an introduction to the fundamental concepts and tools of algebraic topology, including simplicial complexes and the Hodge Laplacian, which are essential to our methodology.
elements.sent-2411-055: Subsequently, we will present our workflow to measure structural similarity between two structures utilizing the Gromov-Hausdorff ultrametric.
elements.sent-2411-056: We will then showcase and discuss results from numerical experiments conducted with molecular data from the fields of chemistry and physics.
elements.sent-2411-057: Finally, we will provide a brief discussion on potential future research directions.

elements.sent-2411-064: More precisely, a $p$-simplex $\sigma^p = \{v_0, v_1, v_2, \cdots, v_p\}$ is defined as a convex hull formed by its $p+1$ affinely independent points $v_0, v_1, v_2, \cdots, v_p$ as follows: \begin{equation}\sigma^p = \bigg\{\lambda_0v_0+\lambda_1v_1+\cdots+\lambda_pv_p \bigg|\sum_{i=0}^p \lambda_i = 0;\forall i, 0\leq \lambda_i \leq 1\bigg\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-065: A $m$-face of a $p$-simplex $\sigma^p$ is defined as a convex hull formed by $m+1$ vertices of $\sigma^p$, where $m < p$.
elements.sent-2411-066: The upper degree of a $p$-simplex $\sigma^p$, denoted by $deg(\sigma^p)$, is the number of $(p+1)$-simplices for which $\sigma^p$ is a face.

elements.sent-2411-069: To facilitate computation, each simplex is assigned an orientation associated with the ordering of its vertices.
elements.sent-2411-070: If two $p$-simplices $\sigma_1^p$ and $\sigma_2^p$ are oriented similarly, then we write $\sigma_1^p \sim \sigma_2^p$.
elements.sent-2411-071: Conversely, we write $\sigma_1^p \not\sim \sigma_2^p$ if they have opposite orientations.

elements.sent-2411-197: In this work, we propose a new framework that employs the cohomology-based Gromov-Hausdorff ultrametric ($u_{GH}$) for quantifying structural similarity between molecular structures.
elements.sent-2411-198: In our workflow, we build simplicial complexes representations of molecular structures and compute the $u_{GH}$ between their respective cohomology generator spaces.
elements.sent-2411-199: The cohomology generators effectively encode the local topological invariants, revealing cyclic patterns formed by edges.
elements.sent-2411-200: We illustrate the application of the cohomology-based Gromov-Hausdorff distance using catalyst data and organic-inorganic halide perovskites (OIHP) data.
elements.sent-2411-201: In our numerical experiments, the $u_{GH}$-based approach demonstrated effectiveness in clustering various structures, while relying solely on the 3D coordinates proved to be insufficient.
elements.sent-2411-202: Our work demonstrates the application of the cohomology-based $u_{GH}$ approach in quantifying structural similarities.
elements.sent-2411-203: However, there are some limitations to our current work.
elements.sent-2411-204: For instance, we focused solely on the cohomology generators of the first-order Hodge Laplacian, which capture loop patterns.
elements.sent-2411-205: In the future, exploring non-cohomology generators may reveal clustering information, and higher-order Hodge Laplacians could unveil more complex structures such as cavities.

elements.sent-2411-214: A central technique in Topological Data Analysis (TDA) is persistent homology, which provides a robust framework for capturing multi-scale topological features in data \cite{edelsbrunner2002topological, zomorodian2004computing .}

elements.sent-2411-217: These geometric approaches have often been shown to provide deeper insights than those achieved through purely topological methods.
elements.sent-2411-218: A promising application of advanced geometric and topological techniques is molecular similarity analysis \cite{bender2004molecular , which is a fundamental concept in cheminformatics.}
elements.sent-2411-219: Molecular similarity measures the resemblance between molecules based on their structure, properties, or function, enabling applications in drug design \cite{vilar2012drug , virtual screening \cite{eckert2007molecular , and reaction prediction \cite{arteca1988shape .}}}
elements.sent-2411-220: By assessing similarity, we can predict properties such as activity and toxicity, thereby accelerating the identification of promising compounds \cite{petrone2012rethinking, basak1998comparative .}
elements.sent-2411-221: Existing methods for evaluating molecular similarity often rely on structural fingerprints, property-based metrics, or topological and 3D shape representations.

elements.sent-2411-225: Recent advances in applied and computational topology and geometry offer powerful methods to assess molecular similarity.
elements.sent-2411-226: Persistent homology and its various refinements and extensions have proven invaluable for capturing molecular topological features in diverse chemical and biological applications \cite{townsend2020representation, xia2014persistent .}
elements.sent-2411-227: However, while effective in tracking the number of connected components, loops, and voids, persistent homology lacks the geometric information necessary to distinguish or identify individual features that arise during filtration.
elements.sent-2411-228: Previous studies have leveraged the connection between persistent homology and dendrograms \cite{carlsson2010characterization, carlsson2008persistent to distinguish structures based on 0-dimensional homology groups, which correspond to connected components.}

elements.sent-2411-238: We present the first workflow to utilize a Gromov-Hausdorff ultrametric approach based on 1-dimensional and higher-dimensional cohomology for assessing structural similarity between molecules, effectively capturing local topological features such as loops and voids that influence molecular properties.

elements.sent-2411-346: We present a workflow to utilize a Gromov-Hausdorff ultrametric approach based on 1-dimensional and higher-dimensional cohomology for assessing structural similarity between molecules, effectively capturing local topological features such as loops and voids that influence molecular properties.

elements.subsection-2411-018: Cohomology-based Gromov–Hausdorff Ultrametric Approach for Structural Similarity Measurement
elements.sent-2411-355: This transformation allows us to compute the Gromov-Hausdorff ultrametric $u_{\text{GH (H^1(K_A), H^1(K_B))$ between two ultrametric spaces associated with molecular structures $A$ and $B$, thereby quantifying their structural similarity or facilitating clustering tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-356: An illustrative figure for the aforementioned workflow is presented in Figure \ref{fig:flowchart .}
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{flowchart.png} \caption{Schematic chart of the cohomology-based Gromov-Hausdorff approach for quantifying structural similarity. (a) Input coordinate data of structure $A$. (b) Construct the associated simplicial complex $K_A$. (c) Compute the cohomology generators. (d) Construct the ultrametric space $H^p(K_A)$ from the cohomology generators represented as a dendrograms. Nodes at the bottom correspond to cohomology generators and they merge at heights equal to their distances. (e) Compute the Gromov-Hausdorff ultrametric $u_{\text{GH}}$ between each pair of structures. Varying filtration values during the construction of the simplicial complex will result in multiple $u_{\text{GH}}$ matrices. (f) Use the $u_{\text{GH}}$ matrices as input to clustering algorithms.} \label{fig:flowchart}
